dateStatements.label: printing of edited text
dateStatements.notes: Morrill's preface says the text was printed in May 1896; the completed volume bears the date 1898.

entities.label: Georgiana Lea Morrill's Speculum Gy de Warewyke (1898)
notes: The edited poem occupies pp. 3–45; critical and explanatory notes occupy pp. 47–102, followed by editions cited, glossary, and an index of names.

Morrill calls the edition non-diplomatic and not distinctively critical: it follows A1 while correcting palpable error, supplies damaged or missing matter, and presents five comparison witnesses selectively in the apparatus.

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous Middle English verse homily now generally called Speculum Guy de Warwick, not the Guy of Warwick romance, Alcuin's Latin Liber de virtutibus et vitiis, any manuscript miscellany, Morrill's edition, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, or a facsimile.

The XML has 1,073 L elements: 1,033 direct poem lines corresponding to Morrill's numbered lines 1–1034 with numbered line 14 absent, and forty witness-only or variant lines nested in apparatus notes. Twelve of those forty form Harley 525's replacement ending after Morrill line 828.

Morrill's six sigla are A1 = Adv.MS.19.2.1, R = Royal 17 B XVII, H1 = Harley 1731, A2 = Arundel 140, D = Dd.11.89, and H2 = Harley 525. Her lost Worsley 67 and empty Bodley 1731 references are not counted as additional extant witnesses.

Complete digital coverage is always scoped to the provider object exposed on 2026-07-12. The NLS project omits physical surfaces; Bodleian exposes only one selected leaf; Manchester's full viewer is not a reusable download authorization.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00039 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse text keyed from Georgiana Lea Morrill's 1898 EETS edition Speculum Gy de Warewyke. Morrill's 1,034-line reading text follows National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.2.1 (the Auchinleck Manuscript) as its continuous base, collates five further witnesses, and supplies its damaged conclusion chiefly from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.11.89. Four additional witnesses now known to scholarship were not used by Morrill. A complete scan of the printed edition and the complete 701-image inventory publicly exposed by the NLS Auchinleck project are cached; the latter remains incomplete as physical-object documentation. One Bodleian image immediately preceding the work is cached as a zero-overlap selection. A complete Manchester viewer is public but was not systematically extracted because the provider's exact terms prohibit that use; no complete public surrogate was verified for the remaining witnesses.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century; attested in early-fourteenth-century and circa 1330–1340 witnesses; independently printed in 1896, with the immediate completed edition published in 1898
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work of English cultural origin; exact place of composition is unknown; Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization is a historical hypothesis
catalogSummary.title: Speculum Guy de Warwick

catalogingSubject.label: Speculum Guy de Warwick
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous early-fourteenth-century Middle English didactic poem or verse homily now generally called Speculum Guy de Warwick. It uses Guy of Warwick asking Alcuin for spiritual instruction as a brief narrative frame around teaching on virtues, vices, salvation, penance, humility, death, and almsgiving. It excludes the Guy of Warwick romance tradition, Alcuin's Latin Liber de virtutibus et vitiis, the ten manuscript codices and their adjacent works, Morrill's 1898 edition and apparatus, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

contentParts.label: Guy's request and instruction in virtues and vices

locators.value: opening through MILESTONE N=164 and following couplet

contentParts.summary: The speaker calls for attention, introduces the penitent Guy of Warwick and Alcuin, records Guy's request for a sermon, and begins moral instruction with virtues and the deadly sins.

contentParts.label: Salvation, judgment, paradise, and the Godhead
contentParts.summary: Teaching on original sin and redemption, the Last Judgment, paradise, and Trinitarian doctrine.

contentParts.label: Penance, confession, prayer, and mercy
contentParts.summary: Instruction on penitence and confession develops into teaching on prayer and divine and human mercy.

contentParts.label: Patience and humility
contentParts.summary: The sermon commends patience under suffering and humility as central Christian virtues.

contentParts.label: Avoidance of sin and preparation for death
contentParts.summary: Admonitions to flee sin lead to sustained meditation on the uncertainty and imminence of death.

contentParts.label: Almsgiving and the widow of Zarephath
notes: The A1 ending is physically defective; Morrill completes lines 1007–1034 chiefly from D, with one word at line 1029 from R.
contentParts.summary: The conclusion urges charity, promises its double spiritual and worldly good, and retells Elijah's encounter with the widow whose meal and oil are miraculously sustained.

assertion.notes: The normalized range operationalizes Edwards's probable early-fourteenth-century dating; it is not a dated authorial event. Morrill's broader 1250–1325 discussion is historical scholarship and is not used to widen the preferred modern statement.

dateStatements.display: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century

assertion.notes: The witness range overlaps the proposed composition period; no exact copying date or priority within the interval is claimed. The Auchinleck witness provides firmer attestation by circa 1330–1340.

dateStatements.display: St John's College Cambridge MS 256 (S.30) is dated to the early fourteenth century, circa 1300–1333

assertion.notes: Morrill says her text was printed in May 1896, while Horstmann's Royal-based text appeared in the 1896 Yorkshire Writers volume. Year-only evidence does not establish which was first, so Morrill's title-page priority claim is not repeated as settled fact.

dateStatements.display: Carl Horstmann and Georgiana Lea Morrill independently printed texts in 1896; exact publication priority is unresolved

assertion.notes: This statement identifies the immediate complete edition used by U-M, not the earliest printing claim.

dateStatements.display: Morrill's completed EETS edition was published in 1898

extent.description: Current scholarship describes the fullest form as 1,034 short-couplet lines, matching Morrill's final line number. The XML has 1,033 direct poem L elements because numbered line 14 is absent; it also has forty L elements nested inside apparatus NOTE elements. Those forty comprise witness-only interpolations, replacement readings, and a twelve-line Harley 525 alternate ending and are not added to the reading-text extent. The XML further contains 4 DIV1, 45 PB, 305 MILESTONE, 1,377 NOTE, and 24 P elements. The NLS project headnote reports 1,031 lines under a different counting description, so all totals remain explicitly scoped.

formStatements.description: A 1,034-line verse homily in short rhyming couplets, with brief embedded Latin quotations.

assertion.notes: The brief Guy/Alcuin exchange frames an extended sermon rather than a sustained romance narrative.
Brief Latin insertions do not make the work mixed-form or evenly bilingual. Morrill's English marginal abstracts and notes belong to the edition layer.

scope.description: brief biblical, patristic, and chapter-heading quotations embedded within the Middle English poem

notes: Work, manuscript witnesses, scholarly editions, electronic encoding, repository artifact, and facsimiles are separate cataloging layers.
Anonymous authorship is maintained: Alcuin is a narrative authority and possible loose source analogue, not the demonstrated author; John Lydgate wrote a distinct Guy poem.
The four witnesses not used by Morrill—Add MS 36983, Manchester English MS 50, St John's MS 256, and Bodleian Add. C. 220—are related sources rather than inputs to the immediate edition.
Thematic content ranges summarize Edwards's analysis and Morrill's lineation; they are not asserted as authorial section divisions.

next_steps: Consult current linguistic and textual scholarship across all ten witnesses rather than transferring any codex's origin or scribal dialect to the work.
Keep early-fourteenth-century composition at medium confidence and exact composition place unknown until stronger evidence is found.
Distinguish composition language, exemplar language, and individual witness dialects in any refinement.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the poem composed, and how much of Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization remains accepted after comparison of all ten witnesses?

next_steps: Compare the Middle English opening directly with a critical Latin text of Alcuin and with likely intermediary florilegia or sermon collections.
Separate the poem's fictional Guy/Alcuin encounter and Morrill's editorial Latin headings from verbal source evidence.
Retain related_to rather than translation_of or adaptation_of until the textual dependence is demonstrated.
openQuestions.question: What demonstrable textual relationship, if any, joins the opening of Speculum Guy de Warwick to Alcuin's De virtutibus et vitiis liber?

next_steps: Find dated publisher records, EETS correspondence, reviews, presentation copies, or issue notices for both 1896 printings.
Do not encode Morrill's title-page claim of first publication as settled modern fact until priority is established.
openQuestions.question: Did Horstmann's Royal-based text or Morrill's May 1896 printing appear first?

next_steps: Collate Morrill's printed pp. 3–45 against the XML and identify the status of numbered line 14.
Document whether each published total includes Latin quotations, missing couplets, supplied lines, and alternate witness endings.
Keep all computational counts scoped to their structural XPath and do not use count(//L) as a reading-text extent.
openQuestions.question: How should the NLS headnote's 1,031-line count, Morrill's numbered 1,034-line text, the 1,033 XML direct L elements, and forty NOTE-nested variant lines be reconciled?

next_steps: Obtain the current institutional description and complete images for codicological and palaeographical review.
Use an updated specialist catalog or direct institutional dating before narrowing the current 1300–1333 range.
openQuestions.question: Can the early-fourteenth-century dating and textual position of St John's MS 256 be narrowed enough to determine whether it predates the Auchinleck witness?

assertion.notes: This is Morrill's localization of the edited textual language, not a securely established place of composition. Current witness dialect distributions cannot be transferred mechanically to the lost authorial text.
Warwick is an association carried by the hero's name, not evidence that the poem was composed there.

scope.description: Guy's legendary identity as earl of Warwick

assertion.notes: The work is anonymous. The fictional or legendary framing of Alcuin as Guy's teacher and later manuscript colophons do not establish authorship by the historical Alcuin; neither is John Lydgate responsible for this poem.

notes: Morrill chose A1 as base, supplied damaged matter, and imposed punctuation, paragraphing, capitalization, lineation, bracket conventions, marginal abstracts, and other edition-layer interventions.
Morrill collated A2, D, H1, H2, and R against A1 but generally omitted merely orthographic or phonetic variants.

subjects.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor

assertion.notes: The legendary figures authorize the sermon. Their narrative association is historically false and is not evidence for authorship or an actual meeting.

subjects.label: Guy of Warwick and Alcuin as devotional authorities
Elijah and the widow of Zarephath

summaries.value: After announcing instruction for the health of the soul, the poem introduces the penitent Guy of Warwick, who asks Alcuin for a written sermon on how to leave worldly temptation. Alcuin teaches the recognition and practice of virtues, rejection of deadly sin, Christian salvation and judgment, paradise and the Godhead, repentance, confession, prayer, mercy, patience, humility, preparation for death, and almsgiving. The final extended exemplum retells Elijah's encounter with the widow of Zarephath to demonstrate charity's spiritual and material reward.

The fullest form has 1,034 short-couplet lines. Guy's request and the opening virtues/vices teaching occupy roughly lines 1–166; salvation, judgment, paradise, and the Godhead lines 167–399; penance and mercy lines 400–568; patience and humility lines 569–691; avoidance of sin and preparation for death lines 692–918; and almsgiving with the widow of Zarephath exemplum lines 919–1034.

The work belongs to the non-romance devotional reception of Guy of Warwick. It is anonymous and is not Alcuin's work: current scholarship finds at most a tenuous relationship between approximately the first 137 lines and Alcuin's Liber de virtutibus et vitiis, with the remainder a free English redaction or independent compilation. Morrill's marginal summaries, Latin chapter headings aligned to Alcuin, punctuation, lineation, and bracketed supplies are edition-layer features.

assertion.notes: This conventional modern title is not attributed to the anonymous author and does not imply identity with a Guy of Warwick romance.
The colophon associates the sermon with Alcuin as its speaking authority; it is not accepted as an authorship attribution to the historical Alcuin.

titles.value: Speculum Gydonis de Warewyke secundum Alquinum heremitam

assertion.notes: This is Morrill's complete reading-text explicit at numbered line 1034, supplied from D because the corresponding A1 leaf portion is lost.

titles.value: Amen. Amen. So mot it be.

work.author: Anonymous
work.dateDisplay: Probably composed in the early fourteenth century; attested in early-fourteenth-century and circa 1330–1340 witnesses; independently printed in 1896, with the immediate completed edition published in 1898
work.regionDisplay: Middle English work of English cultural origin; exact place of composition is unknown; Morrill's Southern-Midland dialect localization is a historical hypothesis

subjects.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment
Almsgiving and care for the poor
Guy of Warwick and Alcuin as devotional authorities
Elijah and the widow of Zarephath

work.summary: After announcing instruction for the health of the soul, the poem introduces the penitent Guy of Warwick, who asks Alcuin for a written sermon on how to leave worldly temptation. Alcuin teaches the recognition and practice of virtues, rejection of deadly sin, Christian salvation and judgment, paradise and the Godhead, repentance, confession, prayer, mercy, patience, humility, preparation for death, and almsgiving. The final extended exemplum retells Elijah's encounter with the widow of Zarephath to demonstrate charity's spiritual and material reward.
